content.value: , armor is critical to staying alive. While it slows you down, as represented by the decrease in agility, it also gives you toughness, which is subtracted from most damage that you take. While it might seem like the penalty for wearing armor is high, in reality that increased toughness means that you are preserving your health every time that you get hit, and this is a massive benefit.

In rare cases it might make sense to maximize your agility so that you can avoid getting hit, and risk that when you get hit you will take a lot of damage. This can be done effectively, but it is risky, and it means that one bad role could be very dangerous for your character. Normally, going into battle without armor is a bad idea. A rogue that likes to sneak around and avoid being targeted might be a character that benefits from not wearing armor.

In some cases, however, armor can be less than helpful. If you are fighting a giant that is swinging a tree trunk as a weapon, your toughness might not be helpful. Subtracting 6 damage from a massive attack that does 4d6 damage is not going to help. If the giant hits you, no amount of armor can save you. In this case, it might be worth shedding the armor and avoid getting hit at all costs.

In other circumstances, such as when you are fighting against small weapons from many opponents, armor can make you nearly invincible to their attacks. Every hit they do might barely do enough damage, if any, to penetrate your damage reduction. When this happens, your opponents are going to have to look for ways to get around your armor. This can be done with attacks that ignore armor such as fire attacks, mental attacks, spells, and so forth.
Artisan armor provides 1 additional toughness, and master crafted armor provides 2 additional toughness

Some armor is considered magical. Magical armor has an associated enchantment, and a number indicated how powerful the enchantment is, such as +7 chainmail of stone. You may spend an eather to use the ability provided by the enchantment. Enchantments may refer to “X”, in this case “X” is the number associated to the enchantment, such as 7 in the above example.
Reaction: Use when you are about to be attacked. Gain +X damage reduction until the end of the current initiative card.
Reaction: Use when you are about to be attacked. Ignore the armor agility penalty until the end of the current initiative card.
Reaction: Use when you are about to be attacked. The attacker takes as much damage as you take from the attack up to a value of X.
Reaction: Use when you make a major movement to move an additional X spaces.
Reaction: Use when you make a major movement to not draw opportunity attacks.
Reaction: Use after being hit by an attack. Deal X fire damage to the attacker that ignores damage reduction.
Reaction: Use when an adjacent creature is about to move. That creature subtracts X from its movement speeds until the end of its next turn.
Reaction: Use after successfully defending against an attack. Push the attacker up to X spaces away from you.
Reaction: Use when you take damage from an attack. Regain X health.
Reaction: Use when hit by a spell attack. Reflect X damage back to the caster that ignores toughness.
Reaction: Use after being hit by a melee attack. Your next melee attack against the attacker gains +X to the attack roll.
Reaction: Use when you are in dim light or darkness and about to be attacked. Gain +X to your defense roll.
Reaction: Use after a melee attack hits you. Deal X damage to all adjacent enemies that ignores damage reduction.
Reaction: Use when you make a major movement. You may move an additional X spaces vertically (fly or jump).
Reaction: Use at the start of your turn after taking damage. Regain X health.
Reaction: Use when targeted by a spell. Gain +X to your willpower defense roll.
Reaction: Use after being damaged. Add +X to your strength for your next melee attack's damage.
Reaction: Use before rolling a defense roll. Reroll the d20 and use the better result.
Reaction: Use when you are about to be attacked. Gain X block against that attack.
Reaction: Use when you are about to be attacked. The attacker has disadvantage on its attack roll.

, shields are the first line of defense. When rolling a defense roll, a shield provides a static chance that you block the opponent’s attack, regardless of the skill of the attacker. Best of all, this happens when you roll low on your agility roll, meaning that when you are most likely to be hit by an attack, you block the attack instead. See the combat section in the gameplay chapter for more information regarding attack and defense rolls.

Each shield will have a size of small, medium, or large, which could be referenced by various game effects. In addition, shields modify your initiative, meaning that you are slightly less quick on your feet than you would otherwise be. However, this comes with the benefit of providing a block value, meaning that when you roll equal to or lower than the listed block value, the attack misses completely. Block values are only used when you are rolling an agility defense roll.

Some attacks might mention that they ignore shields, in which case you would not get this benefit. Examples of these kinds of attacks are area effects, some heroic actions, and large attacks such as a stomp of a giant. In these circumstances, especially the last example, a shield is clearly not going to help!

The negative initiative modifier of artisan shields is reduced by one, and so an artisan small shield reduced initiative by one instead of two and an artisan large shield reduced initiative by three instead of four. The negative initiative modifier of mastercrafted shields is reduced by two, and so a mastercrafted small shield does not reduce initiative and a mastercrafted medium shield reduces initiative by one instead of three.

Some shields are considered magical. Magical shields have an associated enchantment, and a number indicating how powerful the enchantment is, such as +7 medium shield divinity. You may use the indicated ability after successfully blocking with your shield. Enchantments may refer to “X”, in this case “X” is the number associated to the enchantment, such as 7 in the above example.
Move the attacker back up to X spaces.
Do X damage to the attacker that ignores damage reduction.
Gain block X with willpower defense rolls.
Reaction: Use after successfully blocking an attack with your shield. The attacker cannot take major movement on its next turn.
Gain +X toughness until the start of your next turn.
Deal X fire damage to all adjacent enemies.
Gain block equal to X on your next defense roll.
The attacker has -X to its next attack roll.
Choose an adjacent ally; they gain X temporary health.
Your next attack against the attacker gains +X to the attack roll.
Any other attacks the attacker makes on this initiative card are also blocked.
All allies within 3 spaces gain +X temporary health.
One adjacent ally may take a minor movement or make a bonus attack.

, the most common action is the weapon attack. Weapon attacks can be performed during a major action, before or after movement. Other actions will indicate if and when you can make a weapon attack, as detailed in the action description.

Each weapon has a certain cost for the standard, artisan, and master crafted versions of the weapon. Some weapons are one handed, which means that they can be equipped simultaneously with a second weapon, shield, other object, or an open hand. Additionally, weapons will have a size of small, medium, or large. Various game effects may reference the size of the weapon.
Weapons have a range, such as 1 space, 2 spaces, or more. Most melee weapons have a range of 1 space, some will have a longer range. Ranged weapons such as bows will have a much longer range.

Additionally, weapons will modify your base initiative. This means that fast weapons will have a higher initiative and will be more likely to get bonus attacks each round. Slow weapons will likely do more damage but will lower your initiative meaning you are less likely to get bonus attacks. Lastly, each weapon will have a damage dice characteristic which tells you what dice to roll when you hit with an attack.
Small blade \| One handed \| Melee \| 1d4 damage \| Range 1 \| +4 initiative

Bonus Action: If this weapon is your secondary weapon, you may make an attack against the target with this weapon in addition to attacking with your primary weapon. Daggers may act as a ranged weapon that does 2d4 damage and have range 10. Daggers can be stored as a consumable. After throwing a dagger, you may immediately draw another dagger if one is available as a consumable. You may recover these daggers after the encounter.
Medium blade \| One handed \| Melee \| 1d6 damage \| Range 1 \| +2 initiative
Medium blade \| One handed \| Melee \| 1d6 damage \| Range 1 \| +2 initiative
Bonus Action: Make an attack with disadvantage that does an extra 2 damage.
Medium blade \| One handed \| Melee \| 1d6 damage \| Range 1 \| +2 initiative
Gain advantage when both you and the target are adjacent to no other creatures
Medium blade \| One handed \| Melee \| 1d6 damage \| Range 1 \| +2 initiative
Reaction: If you have not yet taken a major action this round, take a major action after being hit by a melee attack. This action counts as your major action for this round.
Medium blade \| One handed \| Melee \| 1d6 damage \| Range 1 \| +2 initiative
Gain an extra damage dice against creatures that have not activated yet in this encounter
Medium blade \| Two handed \| Melee \| 1d8 damage \| Range 1 \| +0 initiative
Large blade \| Two handed \| Melee \| 1d10 damage \| Range 1 \| +0 initiative
Large blade \| Two handed \| Melee \| 1d12 damage \| Range 2 \| -2 initiative
Small axe \| One handed \| Melee \| 1d4 damage \| Range 1 \| +1 initiative

Hand axes may act as a ranged weapon that does 2d4 damage and have range 10. Hand axes can be stored as a consumable. After throwing a hand axe, you may immediately draw another hand axe if one is available as a consumable. You may recover these hand axes after the encounter.
Medium axe \| One \| Melee \| 1d8 damage \| Range 1 \| -1 initiative
Attacks made with two hands ignore shields.
Large axe \| Two handed \| Melee \| 1d12 damage \| Range 1 \| -3 initiative
Attacks made with a great axe ignore shields
Medium blunt \| One handed \| Melee \| 1d6 damage \| Range 1 \| +1 initiative
If the face value of all damage dice are less than the toughness of the target’s armor, ignore the creatures armor.
Bonus Action: Make an attack against a target. If you miss, you may make another attack against a different creature.
Medium blunt \| One handed \| Melee \| 1d8 damage \| Range 1 \| +0 initiative
Bonus Action: Make an attack against a target. If you miss, you may make another attack against a different creature.
Large blunt \| One handed \| Melee \| 1d10 damage \| Range 1 \| -2 initiative
Medium blunt \| One handed \| Melee \| 1d6 damage \| Range 1 \| +0 initiative
If the face value of all damage dice are less than the toughness of the target’s armor, ignore the creatures armor.
Bonus Action: Bonus attacks made with warhammers ignore shields.
Large blunt \| Two handed \| Melee \| 1d12 damage \| Range 1 \| -3 initiative
If the face value of all damage dice are less than the toughness of the target’s armor, ignore the creatures armor.
Bonus Action: Bonus attacks made with warhammers ignore shields
Medium blunt \| One handed \| Melee \| 1d4 damage \| Range 1 \| +0 initiative
Gain +3 from advantage with attacks made with this weapon.
Medium polearm \| One handed \| Melee \| 1d6 damage \| Range 2 \| +1 initiative

You may attack through allies. Spears may act as a ranged weapon that does 2d6 damage and have range 10. Spears can be stored as a consumable. After throwing a spear, you may immediately draw another spear if one is available as a consumable. You may recover these spears after the encounter.
Large polearm \| Two handed \| Melee \| 1d8 damage \| Range 3 \| -2 initiative
Large polearm \| Two handed \| Melee \| 1d6 damage \| Range 2 \| -3 initiative
Attacks made with a halberd ignore toughness that is gained from equipment
Medium polearm \| Two handed \| Melee \| 1d4 damage \| Range 2 \| +4 initiative
Bonus Action: Make two attacks against two different targets, each with disadvantage.
Small ranged \| Two handed \| 2d4 damage \| Range 20 \| +0 initiative
Reaction: Make a ranged attack against any target that gives opportunity within 6 spaces
Large ranged \| Two handed \| 2d6 damage \| Range 30 \| -1 initiative
Reaction: Make a ranged attack against any target that gives opportunity that is 6 spaces away or more
Small ranged \| One handed \| 2d4 damage \| Range 20 \| +2 initiative
After attacking with a hand cross bow while duel wielding you must spend one minor action without moving to reload this weapon before you can attack with this weapon again.
Large ranged \| Two handed \| 2d8 damage \| Range 30 \| -2 initiative
You cannot move during the same turn that you attack with a crossbow.

When rolling damage with an artisan weapon, roll an additional damage dice and take away the lowest result. When rolling damage with a mastercrafted weapon, roll an extra damage dice and add it to the total damage.

Some weapons are considered magical. Magical weapons have an associated enchantment, and a number indicated how powerful the enchantment is, such as +7 longsword of blood. You may spend an eather to use the ability provided by the enchantment. Enchantments may refer to “X”, in this case “X” is the number associated to the enchantment, such as 7 in the above example.
Major Action: Make an attack that does +X damage.
Major Action: Make an attack that does +X damage. The attack ignores innate damage reduction and light armor.
Major Action: Make an attack that does +X damage. The attack ignores damage reduction from metal armor.
Major Action: Point your sword at a target and make an intelligence vs. intelligence roll with +X to your roll. If you succeed you determine whether the target has been honest in the conversation.
Gain X block while wielding this weapon.
Major Action: Make an intelligence vs. agility attack.
Major Action: Make a willpower vs. agility attack.
Bonus Action: Make two attacks against two different creatures. Each attack does +X damage but does not include any other modifiers to the damage roll.
Your first attack each encounter gains +X attack and damage.
Bonus Action: Make an attack with +X to the attack roll.
Major Action: Make an attack that deals an extra X poison damage at the start of the target's next turn.
Major Action: Make an attack that deals +X damage. If it hits, reduce the target's major and minor movement by 2 until the end of their next turn.
Major Action: Make an attack that deals +X damage against undead and demons.
When you do damage with a critical hit, regain health equal to half the damage dealt +X.
When you score a critical hit, do an extra damage dice of damage.
After throwing a weapon, it returns to your hand at the end of the turn.
Major Action: Make an attack that deals +X damage. If it hits, the target’s next minor action is skipped.
Major Action: Make an attack that deals +X damage. If it hits, the target cannot move on its next action (minor or major).
Major Action: Make an attack that deals +X damage and pushes the target ½ X spaces away from you.
Major Action: Make an attack that ignores toughness from non-magical sources.
Gain +X to attack rolls made with this weapon and ignores shields.
Gain +X to attack and damage rolls made with this weapon.
Bonus Action: If you score a critical hit, make another attack against a different target.
After rolling damage with this weapon, reroll one die that showed a 1.
Minor Action: Make an attack against a demonic creature. Gain +X damage on all attacks with this weapon.
Minor Action: Make an attack against an undead creature. Gain +X damage on all attacks with this weapon.
Minor Action: Make an attack against an orc, goblin, or ogre. Gain +X damage on all attacks with this weapon.
Major Action: Make an attack that deals +X damage. If it hits a spellcaster, they cannot cast spells until the end of their major action.
Reaction: When hit by a melee attack while wielding this weapon, deal X damage to the attacker that ignores toughness.
Reaction: When you are targeted by an attack for the first time each encounter, you may make an attack against that creature.
Bonus Action: After hitting with this weapon, grant an adjacent ally +X to their next attack roll.
When you score a critical hit with an attack, make an intimidation vs. bravery attack against that creature that does X damage and ignores armor.

After your third and fifth training in a weapon category, you unlock weapon mastery features of that category. Any time during character advancement where you can choose a race or class feature, you may instead choose a weapon mastery feature from an unlocked category. Weapon mastery features can only be used when wielding a weapon from that category. After the third training you can choose one feature from that category and after the fifth training you can choose a second.
Increase the block provided by shields or weapons by 1.
If you roll a natural 1 on your defense roll you may make an attack back against the attacker.
When you roll a natural 1 on your attack roll, treat the result as a 10 instead of a 1.

Make a ranged attack that does not draw opportunity. The damage of the attack is your normal melee weapon damage. You may immediately draw another weapon but may not attack with it until the next round.
Make two attacks. Each attack must target a different creature.
When you roll a critical hit, ignore shields.
If you have not moved during this round you may choose to gain an extra damage dice on this attack. If you do so, you may not move for the rest of the round.
When you roll a critical hit, ignore all toughness.
When you damage an enemy with a bonus action using a large blunt weapon, do damage that ignores toughness to each enemy that is adjacent to the target equal to your strength.
After hitting a creature that is your size or smaller, you may choose to force that creature to move up to 2 spaces away from you.
After missing with a bonus attack, a single ally may make a melee or ranged attack against the target.
When you roll a critical hit, make a second attack against an enemy creature that is adjacent to the target.
When within melee attack range of a single enemy creature, you may make ranged attacks without drawing opportunity.
Your ranged weapon may act as a melee weapon. It does one less damage dice of damage but adds your strength modifier to the damage result.

If you make a ranged attack against a creature that is adjacent to a single ally and miss, that ally does not need to roll an agility defense roll against your attack. If you have already taken the Close Range Fighting feat, then up to two allies can be adjacent to your target without them having to make a defense roll against your attack when you miss.

Alchemists do not normally use standard coins for currency. Instead, they trade in ether, which they use to perfect their craft and produce potent potions and items that even the most powerful are envious of. Their secret craft is sought to be controlled by all who desire power. Yet, the cunning alchemist guild keeps tight rein on their secrets through their use of archaic languages and encrypted symbolism.

The guild even foes so far as to prevent the sale of potions from anyone other than proper members of the alchemist guild. Kings will help to enforce this rule, as to not lost access to the benefits that the alchemists guild brings to society.

Alchemy items are consumables items purchased with earther. While they will also accept payment with coins for some potions, it is advisable to use ether for purchasing potions while saving coins for higher tier equipment such as artisan and mastercrafted weapons and armor.
Potions can be forcibly given with agility vs. agility or strength vs. strength potion.
Minor action: Heal 1d12 health. The minimum heal is equal to your willpower.
Minor action: In the next round, act on the highest initiative in the encounter deck and may take a minor movement on the enemy’s minor action initiative card, before any enemies move.
Minor action: Gain an extra weapon dice during your next melee weapon attack during this encounter
Minor action: Take a heroic action.
Reaction: After being damaged by a spell, you may use this wardstone to reduce the damage by half.

Minor action: Throw the smoke bomb to a space within 6 spaces, covering all spaces within 2 of the target in smoke. Creatures cannot make ranged attacks into or out of the smoke or make attacks on turns in which they moved into or out of the smoke or take opportunity actions while within the smoke.
Reaction: Add one enhancement to a spell that you are currently casting. You may use up to three spell potions at a time.
Minor action: A creature is put to sleep for 4d6 hours.
Minor action: Move or swap spell enchantments associated between two items.

The mages guild is a loose association of the magically adept that nevertheless wield and retain tremendous influence over the mortal races. Their magic abilities are always sought after whether for war, negotiation, or assassination. The mages guild is always looking to grow in knowledge and learning and so will readily interpret magic scrolls and artifacts. Their scribes will copy the symbols, ancient languages, and mysterious ciphers into their library, while providing their customers with the ability to use the scroll on their own.

While adventuring, players will collect fragments that they can bring to a mages tower or guild house. Each interpreted fragment gives the player a roll on the below table to see what level 1 spell, if any, the fragment provides as a single use scroll. If multiple spells are listed for a single result, you may choose the spell, but the choice must be made as soon as the fragment is interpreted. Multiple fragments can be combined into a single roll. For each fragment combined, the level of the scroll’s spell increases by 1. You can only spend a number of scroll fragments equal to your intelligence. You can also spend additional fragments after the roll in order to modify the result by 1 for each fragment you spend.

There are many other items you may want to purchase.
Torches require one free hand and provide light within 10 spaces of the wielder. They require a major action to light. A character can carry up to five torches in their inventory.

Major action: Make a major movement without drawing opportunity attacks. Choose one creature that you moved past and make a willpower vs. initiative attack against that creature that does 1d12 damage.
A fishing boat holds up to four people.
A ferry boat holds up to 10 people but is not designed to travel on rough waters or at sea.
A sailing boat holds up to 20 people and can travel at sea.
